lens.included_activities: U.S. lower-middle-market operators providing recurring deep-sea passenger or cruise transportation to or from foreign ports for paying external passengers and charter customers.
lens.excluded_activities: Domestic coastal and Great Lakes passenger transport, inland and harbor cruises, ferries, floating casinos, travel agencies, cruise booking platforms without vessel operations, excursion providers, passive vessel-owning or financing vehicles, captive operations, and firms outside the lower-middle-market EBITDA band.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Consumer ticket revenue, charter revenue, and onboard spending for international passenger voyages, sold directly or through travel advisors with advance deposits, dynamic ticket pricing, bundled amenities, and ancillary purchases.

a.bridge: BLS describes shipboard duties across all water transportation, not the 483112 occupation mix, while large cruise-company filings describe data, analytics, pricing, reservations, and customer-experience workflows but do not measure task exposure. I map navigation, engineering, maintenance, safety, housekeeping, food service, entertainment, and in-person passenger care to low-to-moderate substitution exposure and reservations, marketing, sales support, revenue management, scheduling, documentation, procurement, finance, and routine guest communication to higher exposure. Large operators may have more sophisticated shore-side functions than a hypothetical LMM operator, biasing exposure upward.
a.rationale: Passenger shipping contains substantial information work before and after sailing that generative and predictive systems can streamline, including inquiry handling, itinerary content, personalization, pricing support, crew and supply scheduling, document processing, and maintenance triage. Most onboard hospitality and all safety-critical vessel operation remain physical, interpersonal, or accountable human work.
a.change_evidence: Task-level wage and hour studies from passenger operators separating shore-side reservations, sales, marketing, revenue management, administration, and technical support from shipboard hotel and marine departments would materially change the range.
caveats: No cited source provides a 483112-specific wage-weighted task inventory.
Many cruise crewmembers work under foreign flags and may not appear in U.S. payroll data.
The frozen 9.07% labor share may therefore underrepresent labor embedded in outsourced or foreign crew costs.

rho.rationale: Large operators already state that technology, data, and analytics are used for customer experience and operating efficiency, making customer-service, pricing support, content, and administrative use cases implementable. Realization is constrained by fragmented ship and shore systems, privacy and payment data, cyber risk, multilingual guest service, brand quality, labor relations, safety certification, and the need for human exception handling.
rho.change_evidence: Audited deployments at passenger operators reporting baseline and post-implementation labor hours, booking conversion, service quality, complaint and error rates, safety outcomes, integration cost, and sustained usage over at least 12 months would materially move this estimate.
caveats: Observed technology investment comes from global cruise groups far above the LMM band.
No source isolates AI-driven labor substitution from revenue enhancement or general digitization.
Safety-critical automation has a different and slower approval path from shore-side software.

e.rationale: The frozen dataset estimates zero firms in the $1-10M EBITDA band. With no LMM denominator, the share eligible under the lens is undefined; assigning zero would falsely present absence of firms as an observed eligibility probability.
e.change_evidence: A revised firm-level census reconciling the 85 employer establishments to parent companies, normalized EBITDA, control status, related-party operations, and recurring external passenger revenue could establish whether any LMM population exists.
caveats: Census reports establishments rather than transferable firms.
The frozen n value is a margin-bridged estimate, so an actual small niche or expedition operator could be missed.
No replacement or re-estimation of n was permitted.

s5.rationale: A five-year control-transfer probability for eligible firms cannot be defined because the frozen LMM count is zero and no eligible-firm population is established. Large cruise-line brand and vessel transactions would not answer the probability for a nonexistent or unidentified LMM cohort.
s5.change_evidence: If firm-level work identifies eligible LMM operators, five to ten years of beneficial-ownership changes, brand sales, vessel-plus-operations transfers, and owner succession data for that exact cohort would be needed.
caveats: Zero qualifying firms is not the same quantity as a zero probability of transfer.
Vessel sales without customer contracts or operating control are not qualifying transfers.
No defensible denominator-based transaction series was found.

q.bridge: Carnival and Royal Caribbean filings describe dynamically adjusted ticket pricing, differentiated bundled products, advance deposits, and material onboard revenue, and report higher 2025 pricing and spending. These are very large branded cruise groups rather than LMM operators and do not measure retention of AI-generated benefits. I infer that consumer pricing and brand differentiation allow meaningful retention, while competitive discounting, travel-advisor economics, guest expectations, and added service quality share some benefit; large-brand evidence likely overstates retention for a small operator.
q.rationale: Cruise operators sell a differentiated experience rather than a transparent cost-plus service, price cabins dynamically, take advance bookings, and monetize onboard purchases. Those mechanisms can retain productivity benefits, but competitive capacity, promotional pricing, commissions, and the tendency to reinvest savings in guest experience reduce permanent capture.
q.change_evidence: Booking-level price and discount histories, travel-advisor commissions, customer acquisition cost, onboard spend, cancellation behavior, repeat rates, and margin response to prior automation initiatives at a comparable operator would materially change the estimate.
caveats: Both filings represent scale, brand, and loyalty economics unavailable to an LMM entrant.
Reported revenue growth combines demand, capacity, occupancy, currency, and pricing effects.
Consumer protection, refunds, and service recovery can absorb operational savings.

d5.bridge: CLIA reports 37.2 million global ocean-going passengers in 2025 and forecasts 42.1 million in 2029, a four-year ratio of about 1.132, alongside nearly 90% stated intent to cruise again. The association covers the global cruise sector, not U.S. NAICS 483112 firms or a constant-quality service basket, and its capacity-informed forecast may not describe a hypothetical LMM operator. I extend the moderate trajectory to a five-year base ratio of 1.14 while retaining recession and disruption downside.
d5.rationale: Cruise passenger volumes have recovered to records, repeat intent is high, and published capacity-informed forecasts indicate further growth. Demand remains discretionary and vulnerable to recession, health events, geopolitical disruptions, port limits, environmental constraints, and adverse safety publicity.
d5.change_evidence: Independent five-year passenger forecasts by U.S. embarkation port, segment, and price point, plus target-specific forward bookings, cancellation curves, repeat cohorts, capacity plans, and recession sensitivities, would materially change the range.
caveats: CLIA is an industry association and the forecast is global.
Forecast passenger counts partly reflect announced fleet capacity rather than unconstrained demand.
The COVID-19 shutdown demonstrates unusually severe tail risk.

o.rationale: Nearly every surviving passenger voyage still requires an accountable operator to provide the vessel, navigation, safety, maintenance, hospitality, itinerary, booking obligations, and emergency response. Autonomous systems may reduce or relocate marine work, but IMO's framework preserves master accountability and passenger hospitality cannot be supplied entirely by software.
o.change_evidence: Commercial autonomous passenger-ship approvals, actual crew and remote-operations complements, insurer and port requirements, passenger acceptance research, and evidence that platforms rather than operators assume voyage accountability would materially change the estimate.
caveats: This measures the need for an operator, not current crew or shore-side staffing.
A voyage can be consolidated into a larger operator without eliminating operator-required demand.
The autonomous-ship regulatory framework is new and may change during the horizon.

narrative.executive_view: Cruise and deep-sea passenger operations contain automatable booking, marketing, pricing, planning, and administrative work, but the frozen labor share is only 9.07% and shipboard service remains physical. More decisively, the frozen dataset finds zero LMM firms, leaving no established acquisition population and making eligibility and transfer probability missing rather than zero-valued.
narrative.ai_impact: AI can support reservation service, multilingual guest communications, itinerary content, marketing personalization, yield management, crew and supply scheduling, documentation, complaint triage, and predictive maintenance. Navigation, engineering, safety, housekeeping, food and beverage, entertainment, and in-person hospitality remain human- and asset-intensive.
narrative.value_capture: Dynamic pricing, differentiated brands, advance deposits, bundled amenities, and onboard purchases can preserve productivity gains. Competitive promotions, travel-advisor commissions, high guest expectations, and reinvestment in experience will share part of the benefit, especially for an operator without major-brand advantages.
narrative.firm_availability: Census identifies 85 employer establishments, but the frozen size bridge estimates no firms in the target EBITDA band. Establishments can be subsidiaries or offices of global groups, so they do not supply a firm denominator; without contrary firm-level evidence, there is no defensible LMM availability or transfer probability.
narrative.demand_durability: Global ocean-going passenger volume reached 37.2 million in 2025 and CLIA forecasts about 42.1 million in 2029, supporting continued growth and a high repeat-intent backdrop. Demand is discretionary, capacity-led, and exposed to recession, pandemics, geopolitics, port restrictions, safety events, and environmental policy.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: The decisive weakness is the absence of any frozen LMM firm population. Other risks include extreme capital intensity, debt and shipyard commitments, safety and environmental liability, cyber and privacy exposure, fuel costs, port access, demand shocks, foreign crewing, and evidence drawn mainly from very large cruise groups rather than small operators.
narrative.principal_driver: Strong passenger recovery and information-rich commercial workflows create demand and workflow opportunity in the operating industry.
narrative.principal_weakness: The frozen dataset estimates zero LMM firms, so no eligible acquisition or transfer population is established.

sources.title: Census Bureau Profile: 483112 Deep Sea Passenger Transportation
sources.supports: Defines the industry as deep-sea passenger transportation to or from foreign ports and reports 85 employer establishments in 2023 County Business Patterns.

sources.title: Water Transportation Workers: Occupational Outlook Handbook
sources.supports: Documents cruise-ship employment and the physical, mechanical, navigation, passenger-service, recordkeeping, maintenance, and safety duties of water transportation workers.

sources.title: CLIA 2026 State of the Cruise Industry Presentation
sources.supports: Reports 37.2 million global ocean-going passengers in 2025, 20.5 million on typically U.S.-embarked cruises, and forecasts 38.3 million in 2026 rising to 42.1 million in 2029.

sources.title: Carnival Corporation & plc 2025 Form 10-K
sources.supports: Reports ticket revenue as 65% of 2025 revenue, ticket revenue growth driven largely by higher prices, and onboard revenue growth driven largely by higher guest spending.

sources.title: Royal Caribbean Cruises Ltd. 2025 Form 10-K
sources.supports: Describes technology, data and analytics for customer experience and operating efficiency, dynamic cabin pricing, advance deposits, bundled amenities, onboard ancillary revenue, and 2025 passenger and occupancy growth.

sources.title: Federal Maritime Commission FY 2025 Annual Report
sources.supports: Shows a maritime regulator using integrated data tools, automation, and advanced risk management for document-heavy monitoring and compliance workflows, supporting feasibility of analogous shore-side automation.

sources.title: IMO adopts first global Code for autonomous ships
sources.supports: States that fully crewless or remotely operated ships remain limited and that the autonomous-ship framework retains overall responsibility with the master, even when not aboard.
